rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (H2SO4) conditions. This carbocation is then trapped by a simple nitrile, acetonitrile, which after hydrolysis yields the corresponding N-tert-butylacetamide. All components are simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of an alcohol and a nitrile into an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, non-aromatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It specifically avoids complex carbocation precursors, intramolecular cyclizations, or functional groups that might trigger other reaction families like Beckmann rearrangement (oximes), Friedel-Crafts (arenes), or Pinacol rearrangement (vicinal diols).
